items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.q: What is the purpose of a jig in manufacturing?
items.correct: To guide a tool and ensure consistent, accurate repeated operations.
items.misconception: To hold the workpiece very tightly so it does not move.
items.why: Students confuse jigs with fixtures. A fixture HOLDS the workpiece; a jig GUIDES the tool (e.g., a drill jig guides the drill bit to the correct position) - though the terms are sometimes used loosely.

items.q: What force does a rope in a tug-of-war experience?
items.correct: Tension - it is being pulled at both ends.
items.misconception: Compression - because it is squeezed by hands.
items.why: Students confuse the localised hand grip with the force through the rope. Pulling stretches the rope - that's tension. Compression would be pushing the ends together (which a rope cannot resist).
It's Rapid prototyping. “Forging” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Working prototype. “Sketch” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Number of common modelling stages typically taught (sketch, block, working)?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 stages. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
